concepts.title: Variables
concepts.definition: Variables are named containers that store data values. Think of them like labeled boxes where you can put things inside and label them for later use.
keyPoints: Variables have names, like 'name' or 'age'
Variables store values like numbers, text, or true/false
You can change a variable's value anytime
Different languages have different ways to create variables
concepts.realWorldApp: Games use variables to track player scores, health, and position

concepts.title: Data Types
concepts.definition: Data types are categories of data. The main types are: numbers (int, float), text (string), and true/false (boolean).
keyPoints: Integers are whole numbers: 5, 100, -3
Floats are decimal numbers: 3.14, 2.5, -0.5
Strings are text: 'Hello', 'Python', '123'
Booleans are true or false values
concepts.example:
# Python
integer = 42
float_num = 3.14
text = 'Hello World'
bool_val = True
concepts.realWorldApp: Websites use data types to store user information like age (integer), price (float), and username (string)

concepts.title: Operators
concepts.definition: Operators are symbols that perform actions on values. They can add, subtract, compare, and combine values.
keyPoints: Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /, %
Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
Logical operators: and, or, not
Assignment operator: = (puts value into variable)
concepts.example:
# Python
a = 10
b = 3
print(a + b)  # 13
print(a > b)   # True
print(a == b)  # False
concepts.realWorldApp: Games use operators to calculate damage, compare scores, and check game conditions

concepts.title: Conditionals (if/else)
concepts.definition: Conditionals let your code make decisions. You can use 'if', 'else if', and 'else' to run different code based on conditions.
keyPoints: if: runs code only if condition is true
else: runs code if condition is false
elif: checks another condition if first is false
Use comparison operators in conditions
concepts.realWorldApp: Video games use conditionals to check if you have enough health, if you won the level, or if you can use an item

concepts.title: Loops (for and while)
concepts.definition: Loops let your code repeat tasks automatically. You can loop a certain number of times or until a condition changes.
keyPoints: for loops: repeat a specific number of times
while loops: repeat while a condition is true
break: exits the loop early
continue: skips to next loop iteration
concepts.example:
# Python
# For loop
for i in range(5):
    print(i)  # prints 0, 1, 2, 3, 4

# While loop
x = 0
while x < 5:
    print(x)
    x += 1
concepts.realWorldApp: Games loop through all players to update their positions, or loop through inventory items to display them

concepts.title: Functions
concepts.definition: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform a specific task. You define a function once and call it many times.
keyPoints: Define function with 'def' (Python) or similar
Functions can take inputs (parameters/arguments)
Functions can return values
Function names should describe what they do
concepts.example:
# Python
def greet(name):
    return f'Hello, {name}!'

message = greet('Alice')
print(message)  # Hello, Alice!
concepts.realWorldApp: Programs use functions for everything: playing sound, drawing graphics, checking collisions in games

concepts.title: Arrays/Lists
concepts.definition: Arrays (or lists) store multiple values in one variable. You can access individual items using their position (index).
keyPoints: Lists contain multiple items of the same type
Indices start at 0 (first item is at position 0)
You can add, remove, and change items
You can loop through all items in a list
concepts.example:
# Python
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print(fruits[0])  # apple
print(fruits[1])  # banana
fruits.append('date')
concepts.realWorldApp: Games store lists of enemies, items in inventory, or levels to play

concepts.title: Classes and Objects
concepts.definition: Classes are blueprints for creating objects. Objects are instances of classes that bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods) together.
keyPoints: Class: template for creating objects
Object: instance created from a class
Attributes: data that objects hold
Methods: functions that objects can perform
concepts.realWorldApp: Games have classes for Player, Enemy, Item - each with their own attributes and methods

concepts.title: Inheritance
concepts.definition: Inheritance lets you create new classes based on existing ones. Child classes get attributes and methods from parent classes.
keyPoints: Child class inherits from parent class
Child can add new attributes and methods
Child can override parent's methods
Reduces code duplication
concepts.realWorldApp: A game might have a base 'Enemy' class, with subclasses for 'Zombie', 'Goblin', 'Dragon'

concepts.title: Polymorphism
concepts.definition: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. It lets you use the same function name for different types of objects.
keyPoints: Same method name, different behavior for different classes
Makes code flexible and easier to extend
Objects respond to the same message differently
concepts.realWorldApp: All enemies have 'attack()' method, but Dragons attack differently than Goblins

concepts.title: Recursion
concepts.definition: Recursion is when a function calls itself. It breaks a problem into smaller, identical problems until reaching a base case.
keyPoints: Function calls itself with simpler input
Must have a base case (when to stop)
Must make progress toward base case
Useful for problems that are naturally recursive
concepts.example:
# Python
def factorial(n):
    if n <= 1:  # Base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)  # Recursive case

print(factorial(5))  # 120
concepts.realWorldApp: Used in file systems (folders in folders), algorithms (quicksort), and game AI

concepts.title: Sorting Algorithms
concepts.definition: Sorting arranges data in order. Different algorithms have different speeds and use different strategies.
keyPoints: Bubble sort: slow but simple
Quick sort: fast and commonly used
Merge sort: stable and predictable
Python has built-in sort() for easy sorting
concepts.realWorldApp: Search engines sort results by relevance, stores sort products by price, databases sort records

concepts.title: Searching Algorithms
concepts.definition: Searching finds a value in a dataset. Linear search is simple, binary search is fast (but needs sorted data).
keyPoints: Linear search: check each item (slow)
Binary search: divide and conquer (fast)
Hash tables: instant lookup
Choose based on data size and type
concepts.example:
# Python
numbers = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11]

# Simple search
if 7 in numbers:
    print('Found 7!')

# Using index
pos = numbers.index(7)
print(f'Position: {pos}')
concepts.realWorldApp: Google searches the web, phone finds contacts, autocomplete searches suggestions

concepts.title: Design Patterns
concepts.definition: Design patterns are tested solutions to common programming problems. They provide reusable templates for solving problems.
keyPoints: Singleton: only one instance exists
Factory: create objects without specifying exact classes
Observer: notify many objects about state changes
Strategy: choose algorithm at runtime
concepts.realWorldApp: Factories create game objects, Singletons manage game state, Observers handle UI updates

concepts.title: Exception Handling
concepts.definition: Exception handling deals with errors that happen during program execution. Try/catch blocks prevent crashes and handle problems gracefully.
keyPoints: try block: code that might cause an error
except block: code to run if error occurs
finally block: runs regardless of errors
raise: create your own errors
concepts.example:
# Python
try:
    age = int(input('Enter age: '))
except ValueError:
    print('Please enter a valid number')
finally:
    print('Done!')
concepts.realWorldApp: Games handle network errors, file errors when saving, user input errors
